nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and muddy substrates on tropical coral reefs, coral rubble flats, lagoons, and seagrass meadows. Typically observed on the open sand bed adjacent to reefs where it grazes detritus and organic matter from the sediment.
nativeRange.biotope: Coral reef lagoon and back-reef sand flats

decorPreferences: deep, open, mature sand bed at least 5 cm deep with ample uninterrupted surface area
established live rock perimeter that leaves the central sand bed unobstructed
established reef system with mature detrital load before introduction
intake-screened powerheads and overflows to prevent the cucumber from being drawn in and shredded (a common toxin-release trigger)

diet.notes: Continuously processes the upper sand layer, ingesting sand and extracting detritus, diatoms, bacterial films, and microfauna. Generally considered unsuitable for newly established aquariums — the sand bed must have built up enough detrital load to sustain it, otherwise the animal slowly wastes away. Supplementary feeding of sinking algae pellets (staple) and the occasional sinking shrimp pellet has been reported to help maintain body condition in mature systems. Sandbed depletion is a recurring concern in smaller tanks; observers commonly recommend a large footprint of open sand per specimen rather than stocking multiple cucumbers in a single tank.

goodWith: peaceful reef fish (most gobies, blennies, cardinalfish, small wrasses)
soft corals, LPS, SPS, and zoanthids (does not interact with sessile invertebrates)
snails, hermit crabs, and other reef-safe clean-up crew
established mature reef tanks with deep open sand beds
avoidWith: pufferfishes and other species that nip at slow-moving invertebrates
harlequin shrimp and other cucumber/star-eating predators
any system recently treated with copper-based medications
newly set-up tanks lacking a mature detrital sand bed
powerheads or overflows without intake screens

summary: Indo-Pacific detritivorous sea cucumber, generally 20–30 cm at maturity with a reddish-black dorsum and pink underside. Continuously sifts the upper sand layer in mature reef tanks; best regarded as an advanced clean-up species due to sandbed depletion and saponin-toxin risks.
careNotes: Holothuria edulis is generally regarded as reef-safe — it ignores sessile invertebrates and quietly processes the upper sand layer for detritus, diatoms, and bacterial film. It is, however, an advanced rather than beginner clean-up animal for two interlinked reasons. First, like most Holothuria it can release potent skin toxins (holothurin / saponin glycosides) when severely stressed, injured, or killed; in a sealed aquarium volume these toxins are capable of wiping out the entire system. The most commonly reported trigger is the cucumber being drawn into an unscreened powerhead or overflow, so intake screens are not optional. Aggressive tankmates that nip at it (large angels, triggers, puffers) are also a documented stress source. Second, it has a large appetite for a slow-moving detritivore and is reported to gradually waste away in tanks where the sand bed has been depleted — a particular concern in smaller setups and in newly cycled tanks that lack accumulated detritus. The 380 L (~100 gal) minimum used here is biased toward the larger end of the sources' recommendations specifically because of sandbed depletion risk; smaller mature reef tanks with substantial open sand area can sometimes work but are higher-risk. It is highly intolerant of copper-based medications and very sensitive to sudden salinity swings, so slow drip acclimation (an hour or more) is consistently recommended. A dark, almost black-and-grey color phase is sold under the trade name "Black Sea Cucumber" and is the same species; the underside still shows the characteristic pink-mauve coloration. Do not confuse with Holothuria atra (a separate species also marketed as Black Sea Cucumber).

primary.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine — Holothuria edulis species profile
primary.notes: Used as primary because the manifest-supplied LiveAquaria root did not resolve to a species-specific product page for Holothuria edulis (LiveAquaria currently catalogs Holothuria atra as their "Black Sea Cucumber" #703 and a separate generic Sea Cucumber #707, but no dedicated H. edulis listing). TFH species profile provided full husbandry parameters (74–83°F → 23–28°C, SG 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.2, ~12 in / ~30 cm adult size, reef-safe, sand-area requirement, saponin-toxin warning, copper sensitivity, drip-acclimation guidance).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Holothuria edulis
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Holothuriidae, order Holothuriida, phylum Echinodermata), confirmed adult size ~30 cm, native range (Red Sea and East Africa through Indo-Pacific to northern Australia and Pacific islands), habitat (sandy/muddy substrates, coral rubble, seagrass to ~20 m), nocturnal detritivore feeding behaviour, color variation (reddish-black dorsum with pinkish-mauve underside; can also be grey/dark brown — basis for "Black Sea Cucumber" trade-name variant).

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply (manifest-supplied secondary root)
additional.notes: Designated secondary did not have a Holothuria edulis product or care page. Swapped to TFH + Wikipedia for the cross-confirmation per playbook step 3 fallback rule; both confirmed the high-stakes fields.

additional.name: Reefs.com — Aquarium Invertebrates: Sea Cucumbers Part II (Calfo & Fenner)
additional.notes: Returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch but cited via search excerpt — confirmed that H. edulis requires very large (>100 gal / ~380 L) reef tanks with open sand area and that small specimens fare better than large in captivity, supporting the bias-larger 380 L minimum used here.
